title: Anger
lead: Anger isn't the enemy — it's a signal that something matters, feels unfair, or has been crossed. The goal isn't to erase it, but to understand and channel it.
explanation: Anger is a normal, healthy emotion. It often points to a boundary being crossed, a need going unmet, or an injustice. Underneath anger there's frequently something softer — hurt, fear, shame, or grief that feels safer expressed as anger.

The problem is rarely the feeling itself but what we do with it. When anger is suppressed, it can leak out as resentment, tension, or turn inward as depression. When it explodes, it can damage relationships and leave us ashamed.

You can learn to feel anger fully without being controlled by it — noticing it early, understanding its message, and expressing it in ways that respect both you and others. This is a skill, and it can be built.
symptoms: Physical tension, clenched jaw, racing heart
Saying or doing things you later regret
Feeling out of control when angry

immediate.title: Create a pause
steps: Notice the early signs — heat, tension, racing thoughts
Step away from the situation if you can
Take slow breaths with long exhales until the surge lowers
Respond once your thinking brain is back online

immediate.title: Discharge the energy safely
immediate.description: Anger is physical. A brisk walk, pushing against a wall, or vigorous movement helps burn off the adrenaline so you can think clearly.

immediate.title: Look underneath
immediate.description: Ask: what is this anger protecting? Often there's hurt, fear, or an unmet need beneath it. Naming that softens the anger and reveals what you actually need.

longTerm.title: Learn your triggers and patterns
longTerm.description: Tracking what sets you off — and what you were feeling beforehand — helps you intervene earlier, before anger takes over.

longTerm.title: Express needs assertively
longTerm.description: Assertiveness — stating your needs and boundaries clearly and respectfully — lets anger's message be heard without aggression or suppression.

longTerm.title: CBT and anger management
longTerm.description: Structured approaches help you reframe the thoughts that fuel anger and build healthier responses. Free guides are widely available.

resources.title: Controlling anger
resources.whyHere: Evidence-based strategies for managing anger from the APA.
